John opens his gospel with a claim so staggering that it demands we stop and reckon with it. Before any story begins, before any miracle is performed, before Jesus walks onto the page, John insists on something absolute: the Word who became Jesus has always been God. Not a creature who became divine, not a being elevated to deity—but God Himself, existing in perfect communion with God the Father before time ever started. And through this Word, all things that exist came into being. This isn't poetry softening a truth; it's the foundation everything else rests on. John is forcing us to understand that when we meet Jesus in these pages, we are encountering the One who spoke creation into existence. We are meeting God in person.

What makes this announcement even more haunting is verse 10: the world He made did not recognize Him. His own people rejected Him. The God who gave them breath and heartbeat came to them in humility, and they did not know who He was. Yet John shows us that this rejection was not the end of the story. To those who actually received Him—who believed in His name despite His ordinariness—He gave something staggering: the right to become children of God. This is not earned through bloodline or human effort, but born entirely of God. It is pure gift. The same Light that the world's darkness could not overcome offers refuge to anyone willing to look at Him directly and say yes.

The second half of John's prologue anchors the abstract claims in flesh and blood. John the Baptist appears as a witness—a crucial figure who knows his own insignificance. He is not the Light; he points to it. And when Jesus finally walks into view, John's testimony is visceral: "The Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world." Here is the eternal Word made human, and His primary identity in that moment is sacrificial victim. This sets the tone for everything that follows. Jesus doesn't come to rule from a throne or establish an earthly kingdom; He comes bearing the weight of human sin. Then come the disciples—Andrew, Peter, Philip, Nathanael—ordinary people who follow an invitation and discover increasingly that this ordinary-looking man from Nazareth is far more than ordinary. Jesus sees Nathanael before Nathanael sees Him. He renames Simon with prophetic authority. He promises signs and wonders.

In these opening scenes, John is showing us what it means to encounter the Word made flesh: disorientation followed by recognition, encounter followed by transformation, invitation followed by obedience. You will not meet Jesus in this gospel and remain unchanged. The Light has come into the world. The darkness has not overcome it. The only question remaining is whether you will receive Him, or turn away.
